content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

May 5, 1992

Dave Norman
Project Manager
City of Morgan Hill
17555 Peak Avenue
Morgan Hill, CA 95037

Re: Your Request for Informal Advice
Our File No. I-92-170

Dear Mr. Norman:

You have requested advice on behalf of the City of Morgan
Hill's Economic Development Commission relative to their duties
under the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/ Your letter does
not concern a specific governmental decision; therefore, we are
considering it to be a request for informal assistance.2/

QUESTION

Does the Act require the Economic Development Commission's
members to file statements of economic interests?

CONCLUSION

The Economic Development Commission (the "Commission") is not
presently required to file statements of economic interests as it
is not specifically listed in the City of Morgan Hill's conflict
of interest code. However, the commission should be amended into
the city's conflict of interest code and once the amendment is
effective, its members must file statements of economic interests.

FACTS

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section
18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. I-92-170
Page 2


The Commission consists of seven members. Of the seven
members, at least four must be registered voters who reside within
Morgan Hill. No more than three members may, at any one time, be
non-residents of the City of Morgan Hill who are registered voters
in the County of Santa Clara.

The Commission has the power to make recommendations and to
advise the city council in the following matters:

A. The creation of a stronger tax base by obtaining a
greater share of the total industrial and commercial development
in the county;

B. The creation of more job opportunities for local
residents, and making it possible for residents to obtain
employment in the area;

C. Attracting a broad range of industries in order to
reduce labor problems and recent problems concerning the
electronic industries within the north county;

D. Creating and maintaining community facilities that
enhance the quality of life to ensure that the community retains
its labor force;

E. Developing an economic development program that protects
and enhances the environment of the community;

F. Providing assistance and encouragement in the formation
of assessment districts to help lessen the burden of development;

G. Preparing suggested budgets for capital improvements,
and the hiring of personnel to support expansion and development
of the community's tax base;

H. Assisting the city council on such other matters related
to its purposes that may be requested by the city council.

ANALYSIS

Section 87302 provides that conflict of interest codes shall
enumerate the designated employees within the agency who make or
participate in making decisions which may foreseeably have a
material financial effect on any financial interest of the
designated employee. Section 82019 defines a designated employee
as any officer, employee, member or consultant of any agency who
possesses decision-making authority. The term "designated
employee" does not include any unsalaried member of a board or
commission which performs a solely advisory function.

A commission possesses decision-making authority if:

File No. I-92-170
Page 3


(A) It may make a final governmental decision;

(B) It may compel a governmental decision; or it may prevent
a governmental decision by reason of an exclusive power to
initiate the decision or by reason of a veto which may not be
overridden; or

(C) It makes substantive recommendations which are, and over
an extended period of time have been, regularly approved without
significant amendment or modification by another public official
or governmental agency.

Regulation 18700(a)(1).

Regulation 18700(b) provides in part that a designated
employee makes a governmental decision when he or she is acting
within the authority or his or her office, and:

(1) Votes on a matter;

(2) Appoints a person;

(3) Obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of
action;

(4) Enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his
or her agency; or

(5) Determines not to act, within the meaning of sub-
paragraphs (1), (2), (3) or (4).

Applying Regulation 18700 to the types of decisions appearing
in the minutes, the members of the Commission appear to be
participating in the making of decisions. It appears that the
recommendations of the Commission have a significant impact upon
the ultimate outcome of the decisions made by the Redevelopment
Agency, and as such, the Commission is considered to possess
decision-making authority.

You have stated in your March 17, 1992, letter that the city
council has never taken action on a Commission recommendation.
The city has merely solicited the Commission's advice on such
matters as, for example, the scope of work for an Economic Study.
However, the minutes from the April 18, 1991 meeting discuss the
request by the Redevelopment Agency to defer the decision to hire a consultant to the Commission. It appears that
because the Redevelopment Agency could not reach a unanimous
decision they referred this issue to the Commission for
consideration. The Redevelopment Agency then relied heavily on
the recommendation of the Commission to resolve said issue.

Copies of past minutes, specifically August 15, 1992, and
September 19, 1991, respectively, reflect that the Commission
appears to be actively involved in establishing and monitoring the
budget for the Redevelopment Agency. The minutes also indicate

File No. I-92-170
Page 4

that the Commission members carry a fair amount of independent
authority in the decision-making processes of the Redevelopment
Agency. In one instance, Mr. Biggs stated that he will
independently pursue the matter of a railroad undercrossing with
the Department of Public Works to rectify an existing problem.

It would be appropriate for the Economic Development
Commission to be covered by the City of Morgan Hill's conflict of
interest code.37 Once the Commission is added to the code, its
members would file statements of economic interests. The assigned
disclosure responsibilities for the Commission members should be
narrowly tailored to the decision-making authority of the
Commission. (Section 87302.) To assist in this regard, we have
included a copy of a conflict of interest code for an agency
similarly structured.

If it be of further assistance, please feel free to call
me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

[signature]

By: Cheryl A. Hoff
Staff Services Analyst
Legal Division

SH:cah


37/ Enclosed are copies of past advice letters, A-76-191, I-90-82
and A-92-205, each addressing the issue of Economic Development
Commission members as public officials, and the need for those
members to be included in their agency's conflict of interest
code.
